subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit you will become confident with the angle rules that geometry is built on, and you will learn to construct accurate figures using a ruler, a protractor and a pair of compasses. Angles are all around you. When Amina watches the hands of the masjid clock move from Dhuhr towards Asr, when Yusuf works out the slope of a roof in Hargeisa, or when Hassan plans where two roads should meet in Mogadishu, they are all working with angles whether they name them or not.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use the fact that angles around a point sum to 360° to find unknown angles.
Use the fact that angles on a straight line sum to 180° to find unknown angles.
Identify and use vertically opposite angles, which are always equal.
Use the rule that the angles in a triangle sum to 180°.
Identify corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles formed when a transversal crosses parallel lines, and use their relationships.
Measure and draw angles accurately with a protractor, to the nearest degree.
Use a ruler and compasses to bisect a line segment, bisect an angle, and drop a perpendicular.
Construct triangles and quadrilaterals from given measurements.
Solve multi-step problems that combine several angle rules, and give a reason for every step.

concepts.title: Angles on a Line and Around a Point
concepts.explanation: Two rules are the foundation of everything in this unit, so learn them until they are automatic. Angles on a straight line sum to 180°. A straight line represents a half turn. If several angles sit along one side of a straight line and together make that half turn, their measures must add to exactly 180°. For example, if one angle on a line is 125°, the angle beside it must be 180° − 125° = 55°. Two angles that add to 180° are called supplementary.
concepts.example: Angles around a point sum to 360°. Imagine standing at a point and turning all the way around until you face your starting direction again – that is a full turn, and it measures 360°. If several angles share a common…

concepts.title: Intersecting Lines and Vertically Opposite Angles
concepts.explanation: When two straight lines cross at a point, they make four angles. These four angles have a beautiful and useful pattern. The angles directly across the crossing point from each other are called vertically opposite angles, and they are always equal. Let us see *why* they are equal, because in Year 7 a reason matters as much as an answer. Label the four angles a, b, c, d going around the crossing point. Angles a and b sit together on a straight line, so a + b = 180°. Angles b and c also sit together on a straight…
concepts.example: This is a powerful shortcut. If one angle where two lines cross is 65°, then the angle vertically opposite it is also 65° at once, with no calculation. The other two angles are each 180° − 65° = 115° (because they are…

concepts.title: Angles in a Triangle
concepts.explanation: Here is one of the most important facts in all of geometry: the three angles inside any triangle add up to 180°. It does not matter whether the triangle is small or large, thin or fat, tilted or upright – the interior angles always total exactly 180°. Why is this true? Imagine a triangle drawn between three points. If you draw a line through the top vertex parallel to the base, the two ‘tilted’ angles of the triangle reappear beside the top angle as alternate angles (a rule you will meet in Concept 4). The three…
concepts.example: This rule lets you find a missing angle whenever you know the other two. If a triangle has angles of 30° and 90°, the third angle is 180° − (30° + 90°) = 60°. A roof truss shaped like this – 30°, 60°, 90° – is common in…

concepts.title: Parallel Lines and Transversals
concepts.explanation: Parallel lines are lines that run in exactly the same direction and stay the same distance apart forever, so they never meet. On a diagram we mark them with matching little arrows. A transversal is a line that crosses two or more other lines. When a transversal cuts a pair of parallel lines, it creates eight angles, and these angles come in three special families. Corresponding angles are equal. These are angles in the *same position* at each crossing – for example, both in the ‘top-right’ corner. On the page they…
concepts.example: Alternate angles are equal. These lie on *opposite* sides of the transversal, between the two parallel lines. On the page they form a shape like the letter Z (which may be reversed). Alternate angles are equal whenever…

concepts.title: Drawing and Measuring with Ruler and Protractor
concepts.explanation: Accurate drawing is a real mathematical skill, and it rewards patience. Your two everyday tools are the ruler (a straightedge for drawing and measuring straight lengths) and the protractor (a half-circle marked from 0° to 180° for measuring and drawing angles). To measure an angle: place the small cross or hole at the centre of the protractor exactly on the vertex of the angle. Turn the protractor so that one arm of the angle lies along the zero line. Now read the scale where the other arm crosses it. The…
concepts.example: To draw an angle of a given size – say 65° – do the reverse. Draw one arm with your ruler and mark the vertex at one end. Place the protractor centre on the vertex with the arm along the zero line. Find 65° on the…

concepts.title: Constructions with Compasses
concepts.explanation: A pair of compasses draws circles and arcs of an exact radius, and with a ruler it can build precise figures without measuring any angles. These classical constructions, invented by ancient Greek mathematicians, show the logical power of geometry. The golden rule is: do not change the compass width in the middle of a construction unless the instructions tell you to. Perpendicular bisector of a line segment. To cut a segment AB exactly in half at a right angle: open the compasses to more than half the length of AB.…
concepts.example: Bisecting an angle. To cut an angle exactly in half: put the compass point on the vertex and draw an arc that crosses both arms of the angle. Move the point to where the arc crosses one arm and draw a small arc inside…

methods.title: How to measure an angle with a protractor
methods.example: Two angles sit together on a straight line. One is 125°. Find the other.
steps: Put the protractor centre on the vertex.
Line up one arm with the zero line.
Decide if the angle is acute or obtuse, then read the correct scale.
Read where the second arm crosses the scale.

methods.title: How to bisect a line segment (perpendicular bisector)
methods.example: Around a point O the angles are 90°, 110°, 85° and x°. Find x.
steps: Open the compasses to more than half the segment.
Draw arcs above and below from one end.
Keep the same width and draw arcs from the other end.
Rule a line through the two crossing points.

methods.title: How to bisect an angle
methods.example: Two straight lines cross. One angle is 72°. Find all four angles.
steps: Put the compass point on the vertex; draw an arc across both arms.
From each crossing point, draw arcs that meet inside the angle.
Rule a line from the vertex through that meeting point.

methods.title: How to solve a multi-step angle problem
methods.example: A triangle has angles of 48° and 72°. Find the third angle.
steps: Mark every known angle on your diagram.
Find a rule that unlocks one more angle.
Write the new angle with its reason.
Repeat until you reach the answer, then check totals (180° or 360°).

methods.title: Isosceles triangle
methods.example: An isosceles triangle has a top angle of 40°. Find the two equal base angles.
steps: Solution: The two base angles are equal and share what is left after the top angle.
Each base angle = 140° ÷ 2 = 70°.

methods.title: Parallel lines
methods.example: Parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One angle is 125°. Find its corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles.
steps: Solution: Corresponding angle = 125° (corresponding angles are equal).
Alternate angle = 125° (alternate angles are equal).
Co-interior angle = 180° − 125° = 55° (co-interior angles are supplementary).

workedExamples.title: Angle on a straight line
workedExamples.prompt: Two angles sit together on a straight line. One is 125°. Find the other.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°. Missing angle = 180° − 125° = 55°. Check: 125° + 55° = 180°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Angles around a point
workedExamples.prompt: Around a point O the angles are 90°, 110°, 85° and x°. Find x.

workedExamples.title: Vertically opposite angles
workedExamples.prompt: Two straight lines cross. One angle is 72°. Find all four angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The vertically opposite angle is equal, so it is 72°. Each of the other two is on a straight line with the 72° angle, so each is 180° − 72° = 108°. The four angles are 72°, 108°, 72°, 108°. Check around the point: 72° + 108° + 72° + 108° = 360°. ✓

workedExamples.title: A missing triangle angle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 48° and 72°. Find the third angle.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles in a triangle sum to 180°. Third angle = 180° − (48° + 72°) = 180° − 120° = 60°.

workedExamples.title: Isosceles triangle
workedExamples.prompt: An isosceles triangle has a top angle of 40°. Find the two equal base angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The two base angles are equal and share what is left after the top angle. Remaining = 180° − 40° = 140°. Each base angle = 140° ÷ 2 = 70°.

workedExamples.title: Parallel lines
workedExamples.prompt: Parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One angle is 125°. Find its corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Corresponding angle = 125° (corresponding angles are equal). Alternate angle = 125° (alternate angles are equal). Co-interior angle = 180° − 125° = 55° (co-interior angles are supplementary).

workedExamples.title: Angles with algebra
workedExamples.prompt: At a crossing, one angle is (3x + 15)° and the vertically opposite angle is 75°. Find x.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Vertically opposite angles are equal, so 3x + 15 = 75. Then 3x = 60, so x = 20. (Check: 3 × 20 + 15 = 75. ✓)

workedExamples.title: Two rules together
workedExamples.prompt: Parallel lines are crossed by a transversal. A co-interior angle is (2x + 30)° and the other co-interior angle is (3x + 15)°. Find x and both angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Co-interior angles are supplementary, so (2x + 30) + (3x + 15) = 180. That gives 5x + 45 = 180, so 5x = 135 and x = 27. The angles are 2(27) + 30 = 84° and 3(27) + 15 = 96°. Check: 84° + 96° = 180°. ✓

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Two angles on a straight line are (2x)° and (3x)°. Find x and both angles.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A parallelogram has one angle of 68°. Find the other three angles.

workedExamples.title: Name the angle rule shown by each shape
workedExamples.prompt: Name the angle rule shown by each shape: a) an F-shape, b) a Z-shape, c) a C-shape between parallel lines.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: At a crossing, one angle is (4y − 10)° and its vertically opposite angle is (2y + 30)°. Find y and the angle.

activities.title: The Human Angle Clock
activities.materials: an open space and one other person (or your own two arms).
steps: Stand with both arms pointing straight up together – that is a 0° angle between them.
Slowly open one arm out. Stop and estimate the angle, then have a partner check it with a protractor drawn on paper.
Make a right angle (90°), a straight angle (180°), and a reflex angle (more than 180°).
Turn all the way around once and count: you have turned through 360°. Discuss why a full turn is 360°.
This gets the feeling of angle size into your body, so estimates on paper become much better.

activities.title: Straw Triangles Investigation
activities.materials: drinking straws (or thin sticks), scissors, and a protractor.
steps: Cut straws into three pieces and join them into a triangle.
Measure all three angles with your protractor and add them up.
Make three more triangles of very different shapes and measure each one's angles.
Record every total. What do you notice? (They should all be about 180° – small differences are measuring error.)
You have just discovered, with your own hands, that the angles in any triangle sum to 180°.

activities.title: Parallel Lines Angle Hunt
activities.materials: a ruler, a pencil, and coloured pencils.
steps: Draw two parallel lines (use the two long edges of your ruler to keep them equally apart).
Draw a slanting transversal across both lines.
Colour all the corresponding angles one colour, all the alternate angles a second colour, and the co-interior pairs a third.
Measure a few to confirm: corresponding and alternate are equal, co-interior add to 180°.

activities.title: Compass Construction Challenge
activities.materials: a pair of compasses, a ruler, a sharp pencil.
steps: Draw a line segment 10 cm long and construct its perpendicular bisector. Measure to check it cuts the segment at 5 cm and at 90°.
Draw any angle and bisect it with compasses. Measure both halves to check they are equal.
Combine your skills: construct a right angle, then bisect it to make a 45° angle.
Keep your best construction to show your teacher at your next session.

activities.title: Angle Rules Game (Family or Group)
activities.materials: paper cards and a family member or friend.
steps: On each card write an angle situation, e.g. ‘angles on a straight line, one is 130°’.
Shuffle the cards and take turns drawing one.
The player must state the rule and give the missing angle out loud within ten seconds.
A correct rule AND answer scores a point. First to ten points wins.
Saying the rule out loud, not just the number, is what builds real geometry skill.

activities.title: Angles Around You (Real-World Investigation)
activities.materials: a protractor or a phone angle app, and a notebook.
steps: Walk around your home and find five angles: a door ajar, a roof slope, where two roads meet, an open book, a pair of scissors.
Estimate each angle first, then measure it. Record both numbers.
Sort your angles into acute, right, obtuse and reflex.
Find one place where two lines are parallel and something crosses them – describe the angles you see.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity made an angle rule finally click for you? Which construction was hardest to get accurate, and why? What would you like more practice with before the next teacher session? Share your answers at your next meeting.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Basic Angle Rules Angles on a straight line add to 180°. Angles around a point add to 360°. Vertically opposite angles (where two lines cross) are equal. The three angles in a triangle add to 180°. The four angles in a quadrilateral add to 360°.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Parallel-Line Rules Corresponding angles (F-shape) are equal. Alternate angles (Z-shape) are equal. Co-interior angles (C-shape) are supplementary — they add to 180°. These rules only work when the lines are parallel.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Angle Types Acute: less than 90°. Right: exactly 90°. Obtuse: between 90° and 180°. Straight: exactly 180°. Reflex: between 180° and 360°.

terms: The point where two lines or arms meet
Equal angles opposite where two lines cross
A line crossing two or more other lines
Equal angle pairs at parallel lines (F / Z)
Same-side angles at parallel lines; add to 180° (C)
commonMistakes: Around a point use 360°; on a line use 180°
Check the reading matches acute or obtuse

reasoningPrompts.prompt: At a crossing, one angle is (4y − 10)° and its vertically opposite angle is (2y + 30)°. Find y and the angle.
Three angles around a point are in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5. Find each angle.
In triangle PQR, angle P = angle Q and angle R = 80°. Find angles P and Q.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: P + Q + 80 = 180 → P + Q = 100; since P = Q, each = 50°.

questions.question: What does “Vertex” mean?
options: The point where two lines or arms meet
Equal angles opposite where two lines cross
questions.answer: The point where two lines or arms meet
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Vertex means The point where two lines or arms meet.

questions.question: What does “Acute / Obtuse / Reflex” mean?
options: Equal angles opposite where two lines cross
A line crossing two or more other lines
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.

questions.question: What does “Supplementary / Complementary” mean?
options: Equal angles opposite where two lines cross
A line crossing two or more other lines
Equal angle pairs at parallel lines (F / Z)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Supplementary / Complementary means Add to 180° / add to 90°.

selfAssessment: I can use the fact that angles around a point sum to 360° to find unknown angles.
I can use the fact that angles on a straight line sum to 180° to find unknown angles.
I can identify and use vertically opposite angles, which are always equal.
I can use the rule that the angles in a triangle sum to 180°.
I can identify corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles formed when a transversal crosses parallel lines, and use their relationships.
I can measure and draw angles accurately with a protractor, to the nearest degree.
I can use a ruler and compasses to bisect a line segment, bisect an angle, and drop a perpendicular.
I can construct triangles and quadrilaterals from given measurements.
